title: THE RIVER'S GIFT TO THE SEA
html:
Every river dreams of the sea. The mountains, the valleys, the plains — all are just the long road home.

A young river asked an old river: "When we reach the sea, do we disappear?"

"Yes and no," said the old river. "We lose our banks, our names, our shape. But our water does not disappear. It becomes part of something vast."

"Is it frightening?" asked the young river.

"The last bend always is. And then — it is not."

"What does it feel like?"

The old river was quiet for a moment, listening to itself. "Like finally being seen for everything you carried. Every stone you smoothed. Every seed you moved. Every fish you sheltered. All of it — at last — made whole."

The young river listened and flowed on, a little less afraid of the sea.

🌟 MORAL: The endings we fear are often the completions we were always moving toward. Trust the river of your own life.

---

title: THE CHILD WHO TAUGHT THE KING
html:
A great and wise king held court every week where anyone could come and speak. One day a small child of about six years old sat in the front row.

The king's advisors moved to take the child away. The king said: "No. Let the child stay."

After the session the king asked the child: "Why did you come?"

The child said: "My mother told me you were the wisest man in the kingdom. I wanted to see what a wise man looks like."

"And what do you see?" asked the king.

"Just a man," said the child.

The king's advisors gasped at the insolence. But the king laughed — the deepest and most real laugh of his reign.

"Out of the mouths of children," he said. He made the child his unofficial advisor with one duty: to say what everyone else was too afraid to say.

🌟 MORAL: Children see clearly what adults have learned to look past. The wisest leaders keep someone near them who will say the unguarded truth.

---

title: THE THREE GIFTS OF THE MAGI
html:
The wise men who followed the star brought three gifts to the child in the manger.

Gold — because he was a king, and kings were honoured with gold.

Frankincense — an incense burned in worship, because he was divine.

Myrrh — a bitter resin used in burial, because he would die.

The givers did not fully understand what they were giving. They gave what they understood: honour, reverence, preparation.

That the gifts were prophetic — that they told the whole story of the child's life before that life had properly begun — was beyond them.

They gave their best understanding. God used it to say something larger.

This is often how it works: we give what we have and understand, and something greater than us speaks through it.

🌟 MORAL: Give what you have and understand. Do not wait until you can give perfectly. The meaning of a gift is often larger than the giver knows.

---

title: THE KEEPER OF STORIES
html:
In every village in Sierra Leone there is — or used to be — a keeper of stories. Not an elected role, not a paid position. Just someone who remembered.

They remembered the names of those who came before. They remembered who owned which land in the time before papers. They remembered the year of the great flood and the year of the drought and what the people did to survive.

When they died, if no one had listened, those things were gone.

Hawa was twelve when she realised her grandmother was the keeper of stories for their village — and her grandmother was very old.

She began to sit with her grandmother every evening, not to talk, but to listen. She wrote nothing down. She held it all in her heart.

When her grandmother died, the village did not lose its memory. It had passed to Hawa.

She kept it for sixty more years and passed it to her granddaughter.

🌟 MORAL: Listen to your elders now, while they are here. What they carry is irreplaceable, and they are waiting for someone willing to receive it.

---

title: THE LAST STORY AND THE FIRST MORNING
html:
This is the last story of the year. But it is also the first.

Every morning you wake up, you are given a new story to step into. You are the main character. You make choices. Some are small — what to eat, who to greet. Some are large — who to become, how to love, what to build.

The greatest stories ever told — of courage, of kindness, of wisdom, of faith — were all lived by ordinary people who got up one morning and did one thing right.

Daniel went to pray. Ruth walked to a foreign land. Elijah rested and then rose. Joseph forgave. Mariama planted a seed. Anansi let the wisdom go free. The tortoise pushed the sky. The girl listened to the river.

You are a child of Wisdom Academy International. You are a child of Kossoh Town, of New London, of Sierra Leone. You carry stories inside you that the world has not heard yet.

Tomorrow morning when you wake — that is your story beginning.

Tell a good one.

🌟 MORAL: Your life is the greatest story you will ever tell. Begin it with courage, live it with kindness, and pass it on with love. Sweet dreams, and good morning.

---
